note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, in spoken order, split at the script's break tags and sentence boundaries. Block windows come from FFT cross-correlation of each per-step sNN.mp3 against the joined master (8 kHz mono, conj(FFT(sNN))·FFT(master), peak lag = block onset): the Σ(ffprobe audio+0.4) formula predicts 164.155s but the ffprobed master is 163.709s (0.446s off, past the ±0.3s gate), so each block start is its measured cross-correlation onset. Every measured block width equals its decoded audio length + 0.40s to the sample. Final end = ffprobed master duration 163.709s.

phrases.text: Marketing, then Automation.
Open this page when a client asks why a lifecycle text went out, or didn't.
Every automated flow you run sits here on one screen.
Across the top, four numbers.
How many automations exist, how many are live,
how many are still drafts, and how many messages they've sent this month.
Read left to right and you already know the shape of the account.
First panel, Send Volume.
A thirty-day line of daily sends.
When a client says the texts stopped last Tuesday, this curve is where a drop shows up first.
Next to it, Status Breakdown.
It splits your automations into published and draft.
For the real count, trust the cards up top: three published, one draft.
Then Top Triggers.
A donut showing which events set your automations off.
Cancellations lead, recharges and plan upgrades follow.
It tells you where the automated work is concentrated.
Scroll to the list.
Four automations on record.
Two buttons sit to the right, Export and New Automation.
We're only reading today, so leave both alone.
A search box, a filter, a search button.
Type a name to narrow the list.
Browsing changes nothing, so poke around without worry.
Five columns.
Name, the trigger that fires it, when it was created,
when it was last touched, and its status.
That last column is the one you'll check most.
Top row, Win Back, No Visit ninety days.
Its trigger is a customer who hasn't come back.
Status reads Draft, so it isn't live yet.
Nothing is going out from this one.
Cancel Plan Recovery, next.
Trigger's a cancellation, status Published, so it's live.
But look right: weather paused at six locations.
Live and paused at once, which is exactly the answer to a stopped-texts call.
Two more.
Birthday Surprise fires on a member's birthday and runs clean.
First Wash Welcome greets new customers, published, but weather-paused at five sites.
Same story as the cancellation flow.
Status shows as a chip.
Green Published, amber Draft, and that weather note layered on top when a pause is active.
Down the bottom, one through four of four, so the whole account fits on one page.
So, the automation page.
Four numbers up top, three panels for the trends, one table for the detail.
When someone reports a flow that stopped, you can tell in seconds
whether it's a draft, a pause, or something worth a bug.
Where weather pausing gets switched on is still parked.
